data.title: A comprehensive overview of the Web3 Robots market.
data.description:
Robots, once merely mechanical entities confined to science fiction, have moved from laboratories to production lines, logistics centers, and service industries with the proliferation of Web2 architecture platforms and smart devices. While machines have achieved partial automation over the past decade, they remain constrained by closed platforms and centralized control. This is gradually changing with the advent of Web3. Robots are no longer simply hardware; they are on-chain economic agents. The data they generate, the actions they perform, and the value they produce can all be recorded, incentivized, and traded. The combination of AI and robotics is redefining the boundaries between the physical and digital worlds. Below are some representative projects in this field, frequently mentioned in community discussions, contributing to building the future of the robotic economy from different perspectives. OpenMind, hailed as the "Android of robotics," developed OM1—the first fully open-source AI-native robot operating system (MIT License, GitHub 2.5k+ stars, 500+ global contributors). It supports mainstream hardware such as humanoids, quadrupeds, robotic arms, and mobile chassis, providing full-stack capabilities including localization, mapping, planning, remote monitoring, and human-robot takeover. Its FABRIC layer is a decentralized AI control protocol that enables large-scale robot collaboration, trust networks, Zookeeper auditing, machine authentication, and micro-payment incentives (in collaboration with Circle, etc.), allowing robots to share knowledge, coordinate tasks, and form a shared intelligent network. Funding: In August 2025, approximately $20M seed + Series A funding was completed, led by Pantera Capital, with participants including top institutions such as Coinbase Ventures, Digital Currency Group, Ribbit, Sequoia China (now HongShan), and Primitive Ventures. The funds will be used to expand the engineering team, deploy the OM1 robot dog pilot (10 units to be launched in September 2025 for testing in homes, schools, and public settings), and advance the FABRIC network. Token Issuance/TGE: There is currently no official TGE. Tokens (such as OMND) are expected to launch in Q1-Q2 of 2026 (based on investor lineup and roadmap projections). Current Participation Points (Highly Recommended): Season 1 Points Program: Earn points by contributing spatial data to train robot navigation AI; FABRIC Identity Network on Base is now online, allowing you to mint identity NFTs and earn exclusive badges; Over 150k waitlist applicants; Free Mint OpenMind NFTs (requires a small amount of ETH); Contribute to GitHub, participate in OM1 Beta testing, and follow Epoch/Season tasks on Discord/Twitter (@openmind_agi); Join the waitlist on the official website openmind.org to accumulate points, with great potential for airdrops. Konnex: Konnex is a Web3 native, permissionless decentralized marketplace for robot intelligence and physical labor, allowing autonomous robots to sign contracts with each other, trade AI intelligence, and settle in stablecoins, aiming to create "autonomous system GDP". Core technologies include Proof-of-Physical-Work (sensor data audit credentials → automatic payment), Universal Task Language (JSON universal task format breaks down vendor barriers), and Market-Priced AI Intelligence (validators compete for KPIs, and excellent models receive royalties). Application scenarios cover decentralized logistics, robotic kitchens, smart agriculture, etc., aiming to unlock the $25 trillion physical labor economy. Team: CEO Jon Ollwerther (15+ years of robotics experience, 5 exits, over $100M in funding, "40 Under 40"), advisors include drone swarm pioneers. Funding: Just completed a $15M strategic round in January 2026, led/participated by Cogitent Ventures, Leland Ventures, Liquid Capital, Covey, M77 Ventures, Block Maven LLC, etc. Funding will be used for platform expansion, hardware integration, and the development of new verification protocols.

Token Launch/TGE: No tokens are currently listed. The Konnex Points system is now open, and Airdrop has officially launched.

Current Participation Points (Currently the most popular, ongoing): Airdrop is accessed through hub.konnex.world. Connect your wallet to complete Mandatory Quests to unlock more tasks → earn Konnex Points (with promotions such as x1.2x multiplier); the cost is almost zero, and community Daily Tasks are ongoing; it is strongly recommended to participate in quests immediately to accumulate points; white paper at konnex.world/wp; follow up with updates on Twitter @konnex_world.

peaq (PEAQ)

peaq is a Layer-1 blockchain designed specifically for the machine economy, providing machine identities (peaq IDs), on-chain wallets, access control, and nanosecond-level time synchronization (Universal Machine Time), supporting autonomous transactions by millions of robots and devices. Features include tokenized robo-farm (such as the Hong Kong vertical farm pilot) and a Robotics SDK, allowing robots to autonomously earn money and distribute profits; there are already 60+ real-world robot applications.

Funding: Total over $40M+ (including a $15M round in 2024 led by Animoca Brands, Borderless Capital, etc.).

Token Issuance/TGE: Completed, mainnet active, market capitalization approximately $50-60M (fluctuating).

Current Participation Points: The Get Real campaign is ongoing (Season 2+, reward pool 210M $PEAQ, value approximately $100M+), allowing you to earn XP/NP and claim tokens by completing real-world DePIN tasks, quests, and completing tasks. There are still active leaderboards and claim windows; it is recommended to visit the Peaq Get-Real Portal to check in/interact. Additionally, there are Universal Basic Ownership Pilot and tokenized machine deployment opportunities. Virtuals Protocol (VIRTUAL) Virtuals is an AI agent/bot tokenization launchpad that supports on-chain coordination and agent monetization, suitable for gaming, entertainment, and bot scenarios. Its key feature is the Pegasus/Unicorn ecosystem, allowing community participation in agent launches; the Butler tool supports staking and ACP Score ranking. Funding: Multiple seed/IDO rounds, community-driven. Token Issuance/TGE: Launched at the end of 2023, mature, current price around $0.8–1.0, market capitalization $500M+, high-liquidity CEX (such as Gate.io). Current Participation Points: Weekly Epoch airdrop system is active (based on veVIRTUAL staking and Butler usage), 2% to stakers, 3% to ecosystem participants; Virgen Points farming earns points through tasks/holding; ecosystem agent launches often allocate 5% of tokens to the community; Epoch is still running, it is recommended to connect your Base wallet to app.virtuals.io to participate in leaderboard and staking. Geodnet (GEOD) is the world's largest decentralized RTK centimeter-level positioning network (based on Solana), providing high-precision navigation for robots, drones, and autonomous driving. A key feature is its real-time revenue buyback and burn program (80% of data revenue is used for GEOD buyback). Funding: Total $15M+ (multiple rounds from 2023–2025, including one led by Multicoin Capital). Token Issuance/TGE: Completed, migrated from Polygon to Solana (Migration Bonus ended). Current Participation Points: Node operators/data providers can continue to earn rewards; staking and mining reward mechanisms are active; pay attention to offline activities; there are no new large airdrops, but buybacks are beneficial for holding; check node deployment on the official website geodnet.com. XMAQUINA (DEUS) XMAQUINA is a DePIN project, a DAO-governed tokenized autonomous/humanoid robot that provides liquidity exposure and revenue sharing for private humanoid robot companies. Its features include a "Robotics Bank" + Machine Economy Launchpad, with the DAO allocating capital to robot companies (such as Apptronik and Figure AI), and a SubDAO mechanism. Funding: $10.31M+ (3 rounds, including the Genesis Auction).

Token Issuance/TGE: Public sale to be completed by the end of 2025 to early 2026 (in partnership with Virtuals, price $0.06), transferability expected to activate in January-February 2026; 33% TGE unlocked, 67% linear vesting.

Current Participation Points: DEUS holders participate in governance, voting, and revenue sharing; pay attention to DAO proposals, staking/revenue share; Launchpad is coming soon, join the DAO or participate in SubDAO through the official website xmaquina.io.

Robonomics (XRT)

Robonomics is the earliest Web3 bot/IoT coordination platform (2018 beta), providing bot cloud services and smart contract task allocation. Features mature IoT integration. ...

Robonomics is the earliest Web3 bot/IoT coordination platform (2 Funding: Early-stage ICO. Token Issuance/TGE: 2019, listed on Kraken, etc., relatively small market capitalization. Current Participation Point: Network usage/staking, suitable for long-term holding; no recent large airdrops. Other noteworthy projects include IoTeX (IOTX) (mature IoT platform, listed on Binance), Auki (AUKI) (spatial computing), Codec Flow, and other small-cap DePIN tokens. Many emerging projects in the RootData Robot sector are still on testnets; participation is mainly through Discord/testnet. /// ... On-chain identity, incentives, and settlement mechanisms offer new possibilities for machine participation in economic activities. Currently, projects are primarily focused on building underlying capabilities, with large-scale real-world applications still in their early stages. Tokens mainly serve as ecosystem launch tools, and their long-term value depends on actual deployment and usage. Whether bots will become an important part of Web3 remains to be seen. However, since 2024, this sector has moved from a concept to a stage that can be continuously observed. We will continue to track the progress of projects in this sector, the status of TGE (Tracked Objects), and their real-world implementation, and will update and supplement as necessary.

Headlines

Neynar Acquires Decentralized Social Protocol Farcaster, Founding Team Shifts Focus to Wallet Applications

Decentralized Social Infrastructure Company

... Neynar announced its acquisition of the Ethereum social protocol Farcaster from Merkle Manufactory. Farcaster founder Dan Romero stated that the protocol contracts, code repository, official application, and AI token issuance platform Clanker will be transferred to Neynar in the coming weeks for ongoing operation and maintenance. In recent months, Romero and co-founder Varun Srinivasan have gradually withdrawn from the operation of the Farcaster social protocol, shifting their focus to developing wallet applications based on Farcaster. Farcaster completed a $150 million Series A funding round in 2024, valuing the company at $1 billion, but its revenue in the fourth quarter of 2025 declined by 85% year-over-year to $1.84 million.

2. Coinbase

Others believe the acquisition signals industry consolidation, and the founding team's renewed entrepreneurial endeavors and long-term innovation remain noteworthy. 5. X Platform Adds Meme Section Category to "Global Trends" Page

January 22nd news: The X platform recently added a meme section category to its "Global Trends" page, where users worldwide can post various interesting internet memes, funny videos, and other content. Users can also use this page to learn about current mainstream internet memes.

            DeFi Hot Topics

            1. Opinion: BlackRock's Vision of a Universal Blockchain May Refer to Ethereum

            According to Jinse Finance, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink stated at the World Economic Forum in Davos that tokenization is inevitable, and a universal blockchain is the future. If all investments are conducted on a tokenized platform, it will reduce fees and facilitate the seamless flow of capital between money market funds, stocks, and bonds.

            Garrett Jin, an agent for the "BTC OG Insider Whale," stated that it's worth noting that Larry Fink's reference to a universal blockchain may be referring to Ethereum. BlackRock's flagship tokenization fund, BUIDL, is built on Ethereum and supported by partners like Securitize. This effectively indicates that BlackRock is implementing Larry Fink's vision of a "single universal blockchain" and positioning Ethereum as the leading institutional-grade real-world asset (RWA) tokenization infrastructure today.

            2. Celsius Bankruptcy Liquidators Accuse TrueUSD Issuer of Misappropriating Customer Deposits for High-Risk Speculation

            According to Jinse Finance, in October 2025, Celsius' bankruptcy liquidators filed a lawsuit against Archblock, TrueCoin, and TrustToken. These companies previously jointly operated TrueUSD (TUSD) and other "True" series stablecoins, after which the related business was taken over by Techteryx, which is associated with Justin Sun.

            ... The lawsuit alleges that these stablecoin operators invested customer deposits in high-risk offshore investments, amounting to "gambling," and that their partners denied any fiduciary responsibility for the funds. In its response filed in November, Archblock denied the allegations. However, Techteryx, which currently operates TrueUSD, has also filed a lawsuit against First Digital, accusing it of misappropriating escrow funds and investing them in speculative assets. This allegation also appears in the SEC's lawsuit against TrueUSD. The SEC claims that TUSD's dollar reserves were transferred to "speculative and high-risk offshore commodity funds." The SEC's lawsuit also states that TrueCoin and TrustToken, among the companies involved, were aware of redemption issues with the commodity fund as early as 2022.

              3. Justin Sun Makes $8 Million Strategic Investment in River to Support Stablecoin Infrastructure Deployment on TRON Ecosystem

              According to Jinse Finance, River's official statement indicates that Justin Sun, founder of TRON and advisor to HTX, has made a strategic investment of $8 million in River to promote the integration and deployment of River's chain-abstract stablecoin infrastructure on TRON. River's stablecoin, satUSD, can be minted 1:1 with USDT, USDD, or USD, supports cross-chain assets as collateral, and is planned for use in liquidity mining and lending on platforms such as SUN.io and DeFiJUST. The first entry point is native sTRX staking rewards.

              3. Justin Sun Makes $8 Million Strategic Investment in River to Support Stablecoin Infrastructure Deployment on TRON Ecosystem

              According to Jinse Finance, River's official statement indicates that Justin Sun, founder of TRON and advisor to HTX, has made a strategic investment of $8 million in River to promote the integration and deployment of River's chain-abstract stablecoin infrastructure on TRON.

                4. X Platform Announces Top Accounts in Various Industries, Crypto Industry's CZ, Vitalik, etc. Make the List

                Jinse Finance reports that Nikita Bier, X Product Manager and Solana Ecosystem Advisor, stated that based on traffic performance over the past few months, the X platform has summarized the top accounts in various industries and regions. X has compiled them into a new tool called "Starter Pack," designed to help new users find accounts that best match their interests. For the crypto industry, the listed accounts include: CZ, Brian Armstrong, Vitalik, Justin Sun, Shibetoshi Nakamoto, etc.

                5. BlackRock: Ethereum is becoming the "anchor" for Wall Street's tokenization race

                According to Jinse Finance, BlackRock acknowledged Ethereum's dominant position in asset tokenization on Wednesday and dedicated a section in its 2026 thematic investment outlook to discussing the network's potential influence on Wall Street. The report explores whether Ethereum could become a "toll road" to the blockchain financial market, noting that Ethereum "is expected to benefit" as traditional financial institutions, including the DTCC (Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation) and the New York Stock Exchange, advance their related deployments. The report states that currently 65% ​​of tokenized assets are built on Ethereum. Meanwhile, the adoption rate of stablecoins has surpassed that of spot crypto trading, indicating that "tokenized assets may have practical applications beyond pure speculative trading."

                ... Jay Jacobs, head of U.S. equity ETFs at BlackRock (and author of the report), stated that Ethereum, the second-largest digital asset by market capitalization, has the potential for further value accumulation if it is increasingly used by institutions to create digital representations of real-world assets. Jacobs said in an interview, “If we see more tokenized assets using the Ethereum blockchain, it will ultimately benefit from additional trading activity and the issuance of stablecoins or real-world assets.” He added, “If you are an investor looking to participate in the accelerated adoption of blockchain technology, one of the best, and perhaps fastest-growing, use cases right now is tokenization, and Ethereum is a beneficiary of this trend.” While a pie chart in the report mentions 10 blockchain networks capable of supporting tokenized assets, the rest of the text only mentions Bitcoin and Ethereum, suggesting that the world’s largest asset manager holds a more explicit and prominent view on them. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to raise your awareness of risk.

data.title: Who is preventing Bitcoin from breaking 100,000?
data.description:
Summary

  • On-chain structure remains fragile: While Bitcoin prices have risen, the foundation is unstable. Large long-term holders are still hesitant and observing.

  • Supply backlog persists: As prices rise, those who bought at higher prices want to sell and exit, making sustained increases difficult and prone to a sharp drop.

  • Spot market fund flows improve: Some people are starting to buy in the spot market, but they are cautious.

  • Sporadic corporate financial activity: Large institutions are mainly engaged in individual activities.

  • Sparse corporate financial activity:

  • Trader Gamma Positions are Low: Market makers currently don't have enough tokens to act as a "buffer." This means that even a slight influx of buy or sell orders can cause drastic price fluctuations and make the market prone to spiraling out of control.

    • On-Chain Insights

    Over the past two weeks, the anticipated oversold rebound has largely materialized. After reaching the resistance level, the price stalled below the **short-term holders'** cost basis, further confirming significant supply pressure above. Against this backdrop, this report will analyze the structure and behavior of this supply buildup, focusing on emerging seller dynamics.

    Over the past two weeks, the anticipated "rebound after a significant drop" scenario has largely materialized. However, after reaching the resistance level, the price stalled below the cost basis of recent buyers.

    This again proves that there are still many people waiting to sell. Against this backdrop, this report will dissect the structure and behavior of these backlogged sell orders, focusing on who has been selling recently. Looking back at recent on-chain weekly reports, a coherent narrative emerges. The market has been operating in a moderate bear market phase, with a floor price of $81,100 (the market's average cost) and a ceiling at the cost price of recent entrants. This range maintains a fragile balance: there are buyers when prices fall, but every attempt to rise is met with selling pressure from those who bought in the first three quarters of last year (2025). Entering early January 2026, the price rebounded to the upper limit of the range because those who wanted to sell temporarily sold out (no one was dumping shares). However, when the price approached $98,000, the risk increased. Those who bought at this level and were trapped immediately became more active, preparing to exit. Recently, the price encountered resistance near $98,400, almost exactly like the situation in early 2022. Back then, the price failed to rise back to the buyers' cost line several times, resulting in a long period of sideways movement. This similarity tells us that the current recovery is actually very fragile and could be reversed at any time. [Figure 1]

     

    Figure 1

    | Heavy Selling Pressure Above

    Since the price has stalled near everyone's break-even point, a closer look at the distribution of shares will explain why.

    The "Urban Revenue Distribution Chart (URPD)" clearly shows why the accumulated shares above $98,000 are the main culprit hindering the rebound. While the recent rebound has filled the gap between $93,000 and $98,000 (because those trapped at higher prices sold their shares to new entrants), these new shares are accumulating. However, looking at the area above $100,000, there is still a large, dense area of ​​shares, which are slowly becoming "old shares" (long-term holdings). These unsold shares remain a significant hidden risk. If these issues aren't resolved, it will be difficult for prices to break through the 98,400 and 100,000 levels. Unless buying pressure suddenly becomes extremely strong, prices will struggle to surpass them. [Figure 2]

    Figure 2

    | Facing Mature Sellers

    If we shift our focus from short-term buyers to long-term holders, we find the situation is not optimistic either.

    The "Long-Term Holder Cost Heatmap" shows that a large number of long-term held coins are clustered above the current price. This is like a huge reservoir full of potential sell orders.

    Especially when prices rise back to near their original purchase price, they are very likely to sell. Unless new buyers are strong enough to absorb all the sell orders above, these long-term holders are a potential obstacle. As a result, the upside potential is locked in, and any rebound is easily met with selling pressure, unless this accumulated selling pressure is completely absorbed. [Figure 3]

    Figure 3

    |The Runaways in the Rebound

    By digging deeper into the supply side, we can accurately identify who is selling, suppressing the rebound towards $98,000. Looking at the data on "loss liquidation," those who suffered the most severe losses were those who held their positions for 3-6 months, followed by those who held for 6-12 months. This is a typical "painful exit" behavior: most of these people bought in at high prices above 110,000, and now that the price has slightly recovered, they quickly cut their losses and leave the market to reduce risk, not wanting to continue trading. Besides those cutting losses, there are also those taking profits. Those who cashed out their profits explain why there is pressure on the rebound. Data shows that among those who profited, the proportion of those who earned a small profit of 0% to 20% has increased significantly. This indicates that the current sellers are mainly "those who have just broken even" and "short-term traders who quickly enter and exit the market." They run away as soon as they make a tiny profit, instead of holding onto their coins and waiting for a major bull market. This behavior is common when the market is unstable; people lack confidence, prioritize preserving their capital, and run away after making a small profit. It's precisely because these people are in a hurry to sell near their cost price that they are stifling the upward momentum of prices. [Figure 4]

    Figure 4

    Off-chain Insights

    | Spot Fund Flows Improve

    After the previous decline, the spot market has begun to improve. Trading data from Binance and other exchanges shows that the market has returned to a "buying-dominated" state.

    | Spot Fund Flows Improve

    After the previous decline, the spot market has begun to improve. Trading data from Binance and other exchanges shows that the market has returned to a "buying-dominated" state.

    This signifies the end of the previous sustained selling pressure, with spot traders beginning to re-accumulate positions instead of dumping during the rebound. Coinbase, previously a major force behind the sell-off, has now significantly slowed its net selling. With Coinbase, the leading seller, backing down, the selling pressure above has eased, which is helpful for stabilizing prices and supporting a rebound. While current buying pressure isn't as intense as the "frenzied buying" of a major bull market, the fact that major exchanges have returned to net buying indicates that the spot market's fundamentals are much better than before. [Figure 5]

    Figure 5

    | Corporate Crypto Buying: Scattered

    Recently, corporate cash flows remain scattered, mainly driven by individual activities rather than large-scale cryptocurrency accumulation. Although a few companies have occasionally bought a significant amount in the past few weeks, overall, there has been no sustained buying trend among companies.

    In general, cash flows are hovering around a neutral "neither buying nor selling" level.

    This indicates that most companies are taking a wait-and-see approach, or are looking to speculate rather than strategically increasing their positions. This is completely different from the previous scenario where multiple companies lined up to enter the market, driving up prices. Data shows that corporate funds are now playing a marginal role, occasionally joining in, but not yet having a decisive impact. [Figure 6]

    Figure 6

    |The Quiet Contract Market

    Bitcoin futures trading volume has been shrinking, with activity far below normal trending market levels. Although prices have fluctuated recently, trading volume hasn't increased, indicating low participation in the derivatives market and a lack of confidence among investors.

    The current structure indicates that recent price volatility is primarily due to insufficient market depth (poor liquidity), rather than a frenzy of position building. While open interest has changed, trading volume hasn't kept pace, suggesting that existing funds are simply shifting positions, rather than new leveraged funds entering the market. Overall, the current derivatives market resembles a "ghost town"—no one is speculating, and no one is playing. This state means that even a slight increase in trading volume can cause dramatic price fluctuations, but currently, it remains passive and unable to influence the overall market trend. [Figure 7]

    Figure 7

    |Only worried about immediate risks

    The spot sell-off triggered by macroeconomic news and Trump only caused a short-term reaction in the fear index (volatility).

    Short-term volatility increased significantly within a week, but only slightly within three months, and almost remained unchanged within six months.

    This phenomenon of a "sharp surge in the near term" indicates that traders are merely taking tactical action (avoiding immediate volatility) rather than believing that medium-term risks have increased. The fact that only short-term indicators are moving suggests that people perceive this as a sudden event, not a change in the overall environment. The market is paying for short-term risk and is not worried about a long-term collapse. [Figure 8]

    Figure 8

    |Short-term sentiment fluctuates wildly

    Like volatility, short-term "skewness" (an indicator of whether people are betting on a sharp drop or a sharp rise) also fluctuates greatly.

    After balancing for a week, the one-week indicator suddenly and sharply tilted towards put options (more people are buying puts). Since last week, this indicator has been heavily biased towards the bearish side, indicating that everyone is frantically buying insurance to hedge against a drop in the short term.

    The one-month indicator also reacted, but the longer-term indicators remained calm, only slightly bearish. When this indicator tilts this sharply, it usually means it has reached a short-term extreme—everyone is crowding in one direction, making it difficult for the market to continue moving in that direction. Just like after the news about Trump at the Davos Forum, the bearish sentiment was digested, and this indicator rebounded as quickly as it had fallen. [Figure 9]

    Figure 9

    | Options are still very profitable

    The "volatility risk premium" has been positive since the beginning of the year. Simply put, although the current volatility (fear index) is historically low, option prices are still expensive relative to actual market volatility.

    This premium represents the difference between "predicted volatility" and "actual volatility." A positive premium means that those selling options (shorting volatility) are comfortable because they are earning a high insurance premium. This creates an environment favorable to the "sellers," who can profit effortlessly by selling options as long as the actual market doesn't spiral out of control. This mechanism further depresses volatility. As long as selling options is profitable, more people will sell. As of January 20th, this spread remained large, indicating that the current environment is very suitable for shorting volatility strategies. [Figure 10]


    Figure 10

    | Market Maker Positions: The Car Has No Shock Absorbers

    Market makers' Gamma positions can help us see the short-term price structure. Recent fund flows show that buyers are desperately buying downside protection, forcing traders to hold "short Gamma" below $90,000.

    Meanwhile, some people raised money to buy protection by selling options above $90,000, resulting in traders holding "long Gamma" above $90,000. This creates an asymmetrical situation: Below $90,000, traders are short. This means that if the price falls, they have to sell (pump) to hedge, which accelerates the decline. Above $90,000, traders are long. This means that if the price rises, they will sell; if it falls, they will buy. This acts like a shock absorber, stabilizing the price. The result is: Below $90,000 is very dangerous; a slight drop can easily cause a collapse. To stabilize and rebound, sufficient momentum is needed to push up the market makers' position structure. [Figure 11]

    Figure 11

    Conclusion

    Bitcoin is currently still in a "low participation" phase. Price fluctuations are more due to "no one dumping" rather than strong buyer confidence. On-chain data continues to show overhead resistance from trapped investors, and the bottom support structure remains fragile. Although spot fund flows are improving, they have not yet evolved into sustained hoarding behavior.

    Institutional and corporate demand remains cautious, with capital flows stabilizing near a "no in, no out" equilibrium, resulting in highly fragmented trading activity. The derivatives market is also sluggish, with futures trading volume compressed as investors hesitate to leverage. This exacerbates the market's "low liquidity" environment, causing prices to fluctuate sensitively even with slight changes in positions. The options market also reflects this restraint. Only short-term risk pricing has fluctuated, panic hedging demand has returned to normal, and high option premiums continue to keep volatility low, resulting in overall stable market sentiment. Overall, the market appears to be quietly bottoming out. This consolidation is not due to intense battles between bulls and bears, but rather stems from a temporary pause in market confidence—investors are on the sidelines, waiting for the next catalyst to ignite broader participation.

data.title: Probability Tavern: The Seer's Practical Notes
data.description:
Author: Bruce

Foreword

In this age of information overload, we are encountering an unprecedented form of visual deception. Just as the filtered beauty on Xiaohongshu (a Chinese social media platform) often turns out to be a dumping ground in real-world locations, traffic on social media no longer represents reality, and reality often doesn't generate traffic. When truth becomes scarce in the fog of algorithms, how can we cut through the fog to make decisions?

Imagine if every turning point in international affairs, every verbal battle between tech giants, could be quantified into real odds and publicly traded—would the world become clearer or crazier? In a recent in-depth discussion on Day1Gobal, the guests revealed a cruel yet fascinating reality: prediction markets are far more than simple game-theorizing tools; they represent a complete subversion of existing information distribution and trading logic.

Below are five investment principles about truth extracted from this discussion.

Don't be fooled by a 90% win rate; odds are the core of asymmetric opportunities. In the intuition of ordinary investors, chasing projects with an 80%-90% win rate seems like a safe-haven instinct. However, experienced players often see this as a breeding ground for gullible investors. The market prediction isn't a perfect normal distribution; it follows a power-law distribution or the long-tail law. Many people are keen to buy in the 90% win rate range, believing it's a get-rich-quick scheme. But Zixi offers a highly cautionary perspective: the focus of betting isn't the win rate, but the odds. Those seemingly guaranteed wins can wipe out all your profits overnight if a black swan event (like a sudden geopolitical reversal) occurs. True professional players seek asymmetric opportunities: for example, Chess, in the Champions League, saw Manchester City playing away against a little-known Norwegian team, bet on a game with extremely low odds of winning, and ultimately won back $700 with a $100 bet. This logic aligns perfectly with VC investment in the primary market: aiming for high odds rather than focusing on small profit margins. The Victory of Binary Options: Prediction Markets as a Hedging Tool for Your Assets In practical finance, prediction markets are essentially a more intuitive and faster-reacting form of binary options than traditional options. For those holding significant amounts of US stocks or digital assets, geopolitical turmoil means substantial drawdown risk. Zixi shared his real-world example: during a period of heightened tensions with Iran, he invested $3,200 betting on whether the US would take action. The logic behind this wasn't simply to profit, but rather as a hedge: If conflict erupted: the stock account would lose money, but the market prediction position would double, achieving break-even. If nothing happens: a small amount of money in the prediction market goes to zero (premiums), but the majority of assets steadily increase in value in the stock market. This win-win logic allows investors to maintain a calm mindset in volatile markets. Compared to traditional options that require calculating implied volatility (Greeks), prediction markets simplify complex financial models into a black-and-white game, greatly lowering the barrier to entry for ordinary people to participate in risk management. When Radar Becomes a Oracle: Physical Data Leads the Way in Press Releases

The core competition in prediction markets is essentially an arms race of information asymmetry. While the world is refreshing social media awaiting press releases, professional players are using physical laws to uncover the truth.

Zixi revealed details of its profits in the Iran situation: experts ignored diplomatic rhetoric and instead monitored FlightRadar24 in real time. When all commercial flights over Iran suddenly disappeared, or a strange Russian military aircraft was observed flying directly from Israel to Russia, the truth was already clear.

Even more valuable were the exit signals: When Zixi observed the reappearance of commercial flights over Iran, he realized the risk of conflict had subsided, and immediately closed his position. Ultimately, this $3,200 bet yielded $8,000 (a net profit of $4,875), a return of 150%-250%. In the prediction market, physical data (satellites, flight paths, weather) may be the true oracles because they cannot be forged.

Looking at the Cost of Faith Through JJ Lin's App: More Than Just Gambling, It's an Emotional Outlet

Why has the prediction market become a cultural phenomenon? Because it serves as a means for adults to socially vent and discover their faith.

Star mentioned an interesting comparison: JJ Lin's fan app, Linjuli, charges an entry fee of 300 RMB. This shows that people are willing to pay for faith and emotions. The prediction market transforms this psychology into a tool for information discovery.

Whether it's Taylor Swift's chart performance, whether Elon Musk will buy Ryanair, or the Oscar winners, prediction markets offer a low-cost opportunity to participate in grand narratives or entertainment gossip. It turns fan support into a form of real-money logical validation. Winning proves your judgment; losing provides cheap catharsis. This healthy form of catharsis is becoming a new social currency for people under immense pressure in modern society.

Beware of the Power of Definition Black Hole: Don't Sweep the End of the Day When You Have a 99% Win Rate

In the prediction market, the most hidden risk you face is not logical errors, but the risk of the Power of Definition.

Professional institutions have already entered the market. SIG (SIG International), a top global options market maker, has deeply participated in platforms such as Polymarket. These heavily armed competitors even purchase high-resolution satellite imagery to monitor oil production, aiming to gain absolute dominance over the order book.

Even more dangerous is the subjectivity of the judgment results.

Zixi cited an example: during the Trump era, in the decision regarding whether to invade Venezuela, even if the military was mobilized, if the decision-maker subjectively defined it as a special military operation rather than an invasion, then a bet with a 99% win rate could instantly go to zero. Remember: the house not only wins, but the house can also define what constitutes a win. Never blindly buy at the end of an event, because you never know how that last 1% of subjective judgment will destroy your position.

The world will ultimately be a grand game of prediction

Prediction markets are reshaping our interaction with reality. They require every participant to shift from empty talk to paying for knowledge.

... In traditional AMM (Automated Market Maker) logic, we assume liquidity follows a normal distribution; however, in prediction markets, we must deal with the long tail of extreme events. If every opinion you express in the future must be accompanied by a bet amount, would you still easily believe the news you see? In this world where probability and metaphysics coexist, prediction markets may be the only shortcut to truth. Because it understands: money is more persuasive than traffic in making people speak the truth.

data.title: Frequent Controversies Arise: The Black Box of Judgment Power Under the Dilemma of Centralization
data.description:
Author: imToken

Whether the United States "invaded" Venezuela or not—this semantic judgment directly determined a bet worth over ten million dollars.

Does this seem counterintuitive? After all, in the real world, the United States has indeed taken a series of measures against Venezuela, including military deployments and direct action. In everyday language and media narratives, such actions are easily understood as "invasion."

However, the final settlement result was not as some bettors expected—Polymarket did not recognize that the US military's actions constituted "invasion" under its rules, thus denying the validity of the "Yes" option, which triggered protests from bettors.

This is actually a not-so-new, yet highly representative controversy, which once again exposes a long-standing but often overlooked structural problem in prediction markets: When dealing with complex real-world events, what, and by whom, should decentralized prediction markets define "facts"? I. Frequent "Semantic Traps" in Prediction Markets It is said to be "not new" because similar semantic disputes have already occurred many times in prediction markets. Indeed, such situations are commonplace on Polymarket, especially in predictions surrounding political figures and international affairs. The platform has repeatedly issued rulings that users consider "counterintuitive." Some predictions are virtually uncontroversial in reality, yet they have become embroiled in repeated appeals and reversals on the blockchain; in other cases, the final rulings have clearly deviated from the reality judged by most users. In an even more extreme scenario, during the dispute resolution phase, the oracle mechanism allows token holders to participate in voting, leading to situations where top players can "use their voting power to overturn the conclusion" in certain controversial events... These disputes also share a common thread: they are often not technical issues, but rather issues of social consensus. For example, a widely discussed case is the prediction about whether Ukrainian President Zelenskyy "wore a suit" at a specific time: In reality, Zelenskyy attended a public event in a formal suit last June, and multiple interpretations, including those by the BBC and designers, confirmed that it was a suit. Logically, the matter should have been settled, but on Polymarket, this seemingly clear fact evolved into a protracted battle involving hundreds of millions of dollars. During this period, the probabilities of Yes and No fluctuated wildly, leading to numerous high-risk arbitrage opportunities. Some individuals achieved huge unrealized profits in a short period, while the final settlement remained uncertain. The key issue is that Polymarket relies on the decentralized oracle UMA for outcome adjudication, and its operating mechanism allows Holders to participate in dispute resolution through voting. This makes it easy for top players to manipulate the direction of certain controversial events. More controversially, while the platform did not deny the possibility of this mechanism being exploited, it insisted on "rules are rules," refusing to adjust the ruling logic afterward, ultimately allowing large funds to overturn the situation through the rules themselves. It is precisely these kinds of cases that provide a highly representative and clear entry point for understanding the institutional boundaries of prediction markets. II. The Failure Boundaries of "Code is Law" Objectively speaking, prediction markets are now considered one of the most imaginative applications of blockchain, no longer just a small tool for "betting" or "predicting the future," but rather a sentinel for institutions, analysts, and even central banks to observe market sentiment. However, all of this has one prerequisite: the prediction question must be definitively answerable. It's important to understand that blockchain systems are naturally adept at handling deterministic problems—such as whether assets have arrived, whether the state has changed, and whether conditions have been met. Once these results are written onto the blockchain, there is virtually no room for tampering. However, prediction markets often deal with a different kind of objects: whether a war has broken out, whether an election has ended, whether a certain political or military action constitutes a specific judgment. These questions are not inherently coded; they highly depend on context, interpretation, and social consensus, rather than a single, verifiable objective signal. Therefore, regardless of the oracle or adjudication mechanism used, subjectivity is almost unavoidable in the process of transforming real-world events into settleable results. This is also why, in many disputes involving Polymarket, the disagreement between users and the platform is not about whether the facts exist, but about which interpretation of reality is the one that can be settled. Ultimately, when this right of interpretation cannot be fully formalized through code, the underlying logic of the grand vision of "code is law" inevitably touches its boundaries in the face of complex social semantics. III. The "Last Mile" of Truth is Difficult to Decentralize In many decentralized narratives, "centralization" is often seen as a systemic flaw, but I believe that in the specific scenario of prediction markets, the opposite is true. Because prediction markets don't eliminate the power of adjudication, but rather transfer it from one place to another: Transaction and settlement phase: highly decentralized, automated execution; Definition and interpretation phase: highly centralized, reliant on rules and adjudicators; In other words, decentralization solves the problem of credible execution, but cannot avoid the reality of centralized interpretation power. This is why the highly attractive concept of "code is law" in the blockchain world often falls short in prediction markets—because code cannot generate social consensus on its own; it can only faithfully execute established rules. And when the rules themselves cannot cover all the complexities of reality, the power of adjudication inevitably returns to the hands of "people." The difference lies in the fact that this adjudicative power no longer appears as an explicit arbitrator, but is hidden within the definition of the problem, the interpretation of the rules, and the adjudication process. Returning to the controversy surrounding Polymarkets, it doesn't signify the failure of prediction markets, nor does it mean that the decentralized narrative is unrealistic. On the contrary, such controversies remind us to re-understand the applicable boundaries of prediction markets: They are very suitable for data/events with clear results and well-defined structures, but are inherently ill-suited to handling highly politicized, semantically ambiguous, and value-judgment-heavy real-world problems. From this perspective, prediction markets never address "who is right and who is wrong," but rather how the market efficiently aggregates expectations under given rules. Therefore, once the rules themselves become the focus of controversy, the system exposes its institutional boundaries. The recent controversy over whether Venezuela was "invaded" essentially illustrates that when dealing with complex real-world events, decentralization does not mean the absence of an adjudicator, but rather that the adjudicative power exists in a more covert manner. For ordinary users, what truly matters may not be whether the prediction market is decentralized, but rather, when disputes arise, who has the power to define the problem? Who decides which version of reality can be settled? Are the rules clear and predictable enough? In this sense, the prediction market is not only an experiment in collective intelligence, but also a power struggle about "who has the right to define reality." Understanding this allows us to find a balance closer to certainty amidst uncertain truths.

data.title: Devaluation Trading: Why Gold is a Signal and Bitcoin is the Ultimate Beneficiary
data.description:
Author: James Lavish, Unchained and The Informationist; Translated by: Jinse Finance

I. Signals in the Noise

Did you notice the title?

JPMorgan Chase has officially given a trendy name to a phenomenon that has been obvious for years: governments are destroying their currencies, and savvy money is quietly withdrawing.

They call it the “debasement trade.” When Wall Street starts naming a type of trade for its clients, it means two things: the trade is already underway and is about to become mainstream.

But the key to this trend is that, no matter what Wall Street calls it, it is not a “trade” at all.

Trading means tactical operations. Entering, exiting, or perhaps repeatedly swing trading. Seizing opportunities, locking in profits, and moving on to the next opportunity when the trade is no longer attractive.

Hedge funds and trading departments operate in this way: entering, exiting, and repeating the cycle.

What JPMorgan Chase describes, whether they realize it or not, is a more fundamental transformation. This isn't about trading; it's a permanent, structural shift in your mindset about wealth accumulation, saving, and long-term asset allocation. It's an investment philosophy applicable to a world where fiat currencies are systematically devalued. This isn't about market timing; it's about recognizing that the market itself has fundamentally changed. The core insight I want to share with you today is: Gold is the signal, Bitcoin is the ultimate beneficiary. Let me explain this in detail and why I'm so convinced of it. II. Decoding the "Devaluation Trade" First, let's look at JPMorgan Chase's specific wording. Here is their explanation of the drivers behind this trade: "The devaluation trade refers to the trend of increased demand for gold and Bitcoin due to the structural increase in geopolitical uncertainty since 2022, the continued high uncertainty in the long-term inflationary background, concerns about 'debt devaluation' caused by the persistently high fiscal deficits of major economies, concerns about the future independence of central banks, and a general decline in confidence in fiat currencies." — JPMorgan Chase, October 2024 Now let's break down Wall Street jargon and explain it in layman's terms. For those who haven't followed this topic for years, the truth may be shocking. A simplified explanation: "Structural increase in geopolitical uncertainty since 2022" = We don't know what actions governments will take next, and they themselves don't know either. Trade wars, proxy wars, sanctions and counter-sanctions are intertwined. The old rules of the global order are being rewritten. "Persistent High Uncertainty in the Long-Term Inflation Background" = Inflation will not return to 2% and remain at that level. Governments' need for structural, persistent inflation is too urgent. The Federal Reserve is well aware of this. The era of stable low inflation is over. "Concerns about 'Debt Devaluation' Caused by Persistent High Fiscal Deficits in Major Economies" = Governments are spending money they don't have and will continue to do so. The only way to manage such massive debt is through inflation. "Concerns about the Future Independence of Central Banks" = The Federal Reserve will do everything in its power to maintain the solvency of governments. When the alternative is sovereign debt default, the illusion of central bank independence becomes increasingly difficult to maintain. "A General Decline in Confidence in Fiat Currencies" = People are gradually realizing that the dollar, euro, and yen have permanently lost their purchasing power, and this trend is continuing. This is not merely sentiment; it is reflected in actual behavior. **Summary:** Your cash is depreciating; now you need hard assets. **III. Why This Isn't a "Trade"** We need to pause here and emphasize a crucial point. Wall Street likes to package everything as a "trade." This is their way of thinking, and it's how they generate revenue and profit. A trade has entry and exit points, opening and closing positions, repeated operations, calculating profits and losses, settling accounts, and when the trade is no longer attractive, moving on to the next idea. But what JPMorgan Chase describes is not this kind of trade. You can't "exit" a depreciating trade. There isn't a moment when you can say, "Okay, the government has stopped depreciating the currency; it's time to switch back to cash and bonds." It's not like that. What we're discussing is a fundamental reallocation of how you store your wealth throughout your life. This acknowledges that the rules have changed, and the traditional "60/40 portfolio" (allocating a large portion of assets to bonds) is no longer applicable in a world of continuous devaluation. This is an investment philosophy, not a trade. It's a wealth preservation framework spanning decades, not quarters. IV. What is the essence of devaluation? You might ask, "What exactly does 'devaluation' mean?" Let's break it down. The most basic definition is: the government deliberately undermining the purchasing power of its currency. Historically, this was usually achieved by reducing the rim of coins or lowering their precious metal content. In ancient Rome, emperors would collect silver coins, melt them down, mix them with cheap metals, and recast coins of the same face value but with lower intrinsic value. For example, Emperor Nero. After the Great Fire of Rome in 64 AD, he collected Roman silver coins, melted them down, mixed them with base metals, and recast coins that were lighter but had the same face value. This seemingly ingenious short-term budget solution was in fact a slow plunder of Roman savers, ultimately eroding trust in the currency and even the emperor himself. Modern devaluation is more complex, at least in form, and more covert because you can't see it happening in real time like observing a coin being chipped. In the United States, for example, modern devaluation is primarily achieved through excessive government spending driven by the Treasury and supported by the Federal Reserve. Furthermore, credit expansion in the banking system, financial repression through negative real interest rates, and crisis relief programs financed with newly created money all exacerbate devaluation. The result is that every dollar you hold loses purchasing power over time. The key point is that this is not a temporary, reversible situation. Governments need sustained inflation to keep their debt burden manageable. But the harsh reality is: No fiat currency can maintain its purchasing power in the long run, without exception. This is not an exaggeration. Depending on the definition of "failure," hundreds to thousands of fiat currencies have collapsed throughout history. While estimates vary, dozens perished due to hyperinflation, and many more were gradually phased out through slow devaluation. The "graveyard" of failed currencies is vast. The French Revolution's post-revolutionary notes became worthless within seven years; the Continental coin—you may have heard the phrase "not worth a Continental"; the Weimar Republic's Mark—Germans preferred burning money for heating because the currency was cheaper than firewood; the Hungarian Pengő—experienced the worst hyperinflation on record, with prices doubling every 15 hours; the Zimbabwean dollar—issued a ridiculous 100 trillion dollar note; the Venezuelan Bolivar—devalued by 99.99%, forcing millions to flee their homes. The graveyard of fiat currencies: without exception, no fiat currency can maintain its purchasing power in the long run. These are just some of the dramatic collapses. Dozens more currencies have quietly disappeared through revaluation, replacement, or slow erosion. Argentina has changed its currency five times since 1970, and Brazil eight times. The pattern remains consistent: government printing money, currency collapse, and the people suffer. Note: Roman silver coins were not strictly legal tender, but rather silver coins with real intrinsic value. However, emperors still devalued them, reducing their silver content from 95% pure silver to less than 5% over three centuries. The lesson is: governments can always find ways to devalue their currencies, and fiat currency merely removes the obstacle to this process. What took Rome 500 years to accomplish, can now be done in a mere ten. And what about the "survivors"? The British pound, in circulation since 1694, is one of the oldest existing currencies—a success story? Not at all. Since 1914, the pound has lost 99.5% of its purchasing power. Today, one pound has the equivalent of only one penny a century ago. It didn't collapse in hyperinflation, but rather was slowly eroded decade after decade by the Bank of England's printing money and devaluation. The "survivors" are not success stories, just slower to disappear. The dollar, having become legal tender only 54 years after its complete separation from gold, has lost 87% of its purchasing power in the past. This lifespan exceeds the average lifespan of a legal tender. And those who issue this currency are telling you their true vision for its future through their actions. The question is not whether fiat currency will fail, but how it will fail: through rapid collapse via hyperinflation or through slow demise via sustained devaluation. Either way, the outcome is the same. Unlike past devaluations that spanned decades or centuries, this devaluation has a clear time urgency. To understand the reasons and methods, let's look at the relevant data. V. Key Data Determining a Permanent Transformation Many of you may have heard me discuss this topic before. But for today's subject, a brief recap is worthwhile, as these data demonstrate that this is not a tactical transaction, but a structural transformation without an exit mechanism. By the end of 2025, US federal debt will exceed $38.4 trillion. This represents an increase of over $7 trillion in just three years. Historically, it took the US 229 years, from 1776 to 2005, to accumulate its initial $7 trillion debt. And we added the same amount of debt in just three years. But the real problem isn't the debt itself, but the cost of repaying it. Annual interest payments have soared to over $1.1 trillion, becoming the second-largest expenditure in the federal budget after Social Security. We pay about $3 billion a day in interest alone, not including principal. Real-time Debt Spiral: Federal Debt and Net Interest (2015-2025) This is a true depiction of the debt spiral: increased debt leads to higher interest rates, higher interest rates lead to higher interest payments, higher interest payments lead to a larger deficit, and a larger deficit requires even more debt financing. The cycle repeats itself, escalating ever more. Interest expenses devour the budget: In just four years, interest expenses jumped from the fifth largest budget item to the second. Within just three years, interest expenses jumped from the fifth largest budget item to the second. VI. No Third Option So, what will the Federal Reserve do? Option A: Maintain high interest rates to combat inflation. But this will lead to a further spiral of rising interest expenses, an explosive increase in the deficit, and stagnant economic growth. Ultimately, the bond market will stagnate, forcing a shift to Option B regardless. Option B: Cut interest rates and restart quantitative easing (QE). Inflation will return, and the purchasing power of the dollar will continue to erode. However, the government can use a depreciated dollar to repay its debt. This is the path of least resistance. Option C, which "solves" the problem through fiscal austerity, does not exist. This is politically suicidal and will never be implemented. Furthermore, the Treasury's own data clearly shows the future direction: the U.S. Treasury predicts that, under its hypothetical scenario, the debt-to-GDP ratio will exceed 200% by 2049 and reach 535% by 2099. Therefore, the only option left is a fiscally unfavorable path.

All paths ultimately point to currency devaluation.

The truth is, devaluation is not a prediction, but an understanding that the data has already determined the outcome. And that data will not change.

This is why calling it a "trade" is completely off-topic. You can't trade around a permanent situation; you have to adjust your entire investment philosophy to fit it.

This raises a disturbing question: if devaluation is inevitable, what fate awaits the traditional portfolios still held by many long-term investors and private clients?

VII. The Demise of the 60/40 Portfolio

Why the “Balanced” Portfolio Is Now Inevitably Losing

For decades, the 60/40 portfolio has been the gold standard for prudent investing: 60% allocated to stocks for growth and 40% to bonds for protection. Simple, sophisticated, and Nobel Prize-winning.

But 2022 changed everything.

The 60/40 portfolio lost 17.5%, its worst performance since 1937 and the fourth worst annual performance in 200 years. Stocks fell 19%, the composite bond index fell 13%, and long-term government bonds, on which many retirees rely for income, plummeted 31%. The so-called “balanced” portfolio was completely out of balance.

But worryingly, 2022 was not an anomaly, but rather a harbinger of future trends.

According to Morningstar's 150-year analysis, 2022 was the only year in 150 years during a stock market downturn that bonds failed to provide any risk diversification benefits. Only once in a century and a half. What exactly changed? Everything changed. The End of a 40-Year Tailwind Look at the chart of 10-year U.S. Treasury yields since 1962.

10-Year US Treasury Yield (1962-2025)

- Phase 1: Rising Interest Rates (1962-1981). Yields climbed from 4% to a peak of 15.84% in September 1981. Bonds were a bad investment; rising interest rates led to falling prices.

- Phase 2: A 40-Year Bond Bull Market (1981-2020). This era created the 60/40 portfolio and fixed-income legends like Bill Gross of Pacific Investment Management Company (PIMCO) (“Bond King”).

Why? Yields fell continuously from 16% to 0.65%, a decline that lasted for forty years. As interest rates fell, bond prices rose, allowing investors to earn both interest income and capital appreciation. The 60/40 portfolio was not only "balanced" but also gained super-strong growth momentum due to its consistent profitability from a one-way bet. - Phase Three: The New Paradigm (2020 to Present). The trend line was broken, and yields soared from 0.65% to over 4%. The forty-year tailwind has turned into a headwind. Many investors have never understood that the brilliant performance of the 60/40 portfolio was a product of a specific and unrepeatable era. This stemmed not only from the negative correlation between stocks and bonds but also from the forty-year bull market in bonds, which provided capital appreciation in addition to interest. That era is over. Interest rates cannot fall from 16% to zero again; they are already at extremely low levels. The logic that once made the 60/40 portfolio so successful no longer applies.

Correlation Reversal

Besides the end of the bond bull market, a more fundamental change has occurred.

The 60/40 portfolio worked because historically, stocks and bonds moved inversely. When the stock market crashed, investors flocked to bonds for safety, driving up bond prices. This negative correlation was the cornerstone of modern portfolio theory.

But this negative correlation was a product of a specific paradigm, which has now ended.

From 2000 to 2021, the correlation between stocks and bonds was consistently negative, making bonds a reliable hedging tool. But in 2022, this correlation turned to +0.60, meaning that stocks and bonds moved in tandem, with some indicators even reaching +0.70.

The table below tells you the truth.

Two Worlds of the 60/40 Portfolio

Bonds played their proper role during growth shocks such as the bursting of the dot-com bubble, the global financial crisis, and the COVID-19 pandemic, buffering losses. The COVID-19 crash had almost no impact on the 60/40 portfolio.

But during inflationary shocks? Disaster struck. Stagflation in the 1970s caused a 39.4% drawdown in the portfolio, and in 2022, the mixed portfolio saw a 25.1% drawdown. Particularly painfully, bonds not only failed to buffer losses but actually exacerbated them.

BlackRock's research clearly shows that bonds can hedge against growth shocks, but not inflation shocks. When the economy slows, the Fed cuts interest rates, bond prices rise, and stock losses are buffered. But when inflation becomes a problem, the Fed raises interest rates, simultaneously damaging both the stock and bond markets. VIII. Why This is Structural, Not Cyclical This is where the devaluation trade argument directly relates to your portfolio. We've already discussed the relevant data: the debt spiral, the explosive growth in interest payments, and the Fed's dilemma. The question isn't whether inflation will be high, but whether the government can afford the consequences of low inflation. The answer is clearly no. Economists call this "financial repression": keeping interest rates below inflation levels for an extended period to slowly "liquidate" debt. This isn't theory, but historical fact. After World War II, the real yield on U.S. government debt was negative in about half the years. This "hidden tax" on savers reduced the real value of U.S. debt by about 3-4% of GDP annually. Combined with strong economic growth, this helped reduce the ratio of total federal debt to GDP from about 120% in 1946 to 60% in the mid-1950s, and further to about 30% in the mid-1970s. Today, this strategy remains the only viable path forward. If the government needs inflation to exceed interest rates, then bonds cannot protect your portfolio. This is not a temporary situation, but a structural inevitability. How it works: When your bond yield is 4% and the inflation rate is 5%, your purchasing power will inevitably lose 1% annually. As deficit spending continues, inflation will inevitably surge, and both your stocks and bonds will suffer. The 60/40 portfolio performed exceptionally well over the past four decades because we were in a specific paradigm: declining inflation, declining interest rates, a negative correlation between stocks and bonds, and a bond bull market. Today, we are entering an era of structural inflation, financial repression, positive correlation, and no tailwinds for declining bond yields. In this environment, bonds cannot hedge risk and will only continue to deplete wealth. New Risk Diversification Tools If bonds cannot hedge inflation, what can? Assets with inelastic supply: those that cannot be eroded by printing money, devaluation, or inflation. This is precisely why central banks have been continuously increasing their gold reserves recently. They understand the impending changes. This is also why institutions like BlackRock and the Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund include Bitcoin alongside gold in their asset allocations. Both assets exist outside of systems that require devaluation. The traditional view that portfolios should be "balanced" by allocating bonds is now actually more harmful than helpful. You not only failed to reduce risk, but you also ensured that your wealth suffered long-term losses due to inflation, while simultaneously forfeiting potential compensatory gains. In a world of financial repression, hard assets are not only opportunities, but also necessities. Therefore, it is not surprising how those who actually print money allocate their reserve assets. IX. Gold: A Signal Hard assets are the answer, but how can we be sure this is not just theory? Look at the actual actions of those who print money. Interestingly, while most investors are still debating whether devaluation is real, a group of participants has already made their decisions. And they should be the ones who know the most. They are central banks. These money-printing institutions are buying gold at the fastest pace in decades, and without any concealment. Central banks currently hold nearly 38,000 tons of gold, worth over $5 trillion at current prices. This represents about one-fifth of the total gold mined in human history, stored in the vaults of governments worldwide. From 2010 to 2024, central banks have made net purchases of over 1,000 tons of gold annually for three consecutive years. Previously, purchasing 500 tons annually was considered a large-scale purchase. According to the World Gold Council's 2025 survey, 43% of central banks plan to increase their gold reserves in the next 12 months, up from 29% last year. Meanwhile, a staggering 95% of central banks expect global central bank reserves to increase, up from 81% last year. Please read this again: Nearly half of central banks plan to increase their gold reserves, and almost all central banks expect this trend to continue. When the printers of money start buying gold, they are telling you an important message about the currency they are printing. This is why we see gold as a signal. Central banks are not speculating or day trading; they are making structural, long-term asset allocation decisions. They are reallocating their reserve assets for a world of currency devaluation. It's important to note: they are not "trading," but permanently increasing their holdings. The US dollar's share of global foreign exchange reserves has fallen to approximately 57.8%, a 20-year low. Gold's share has risen to 18%, a multi-decade high. Central banks are not only buying gold but also actively reducing their dollar exposure. Gold is projected to return over 60% by 2025, trading above $4,500 per ounce, more than double what it was a few years ago. This is not speculation or a bubble, but a vote by the world's most sophisticated monetary institutions with their reserve assets. They are clearly telling you what's coming. This is not speculation or a bubble, but a vote by the world's most sophisticated monetary institutions with their reserve assets. They are clearly telling you what's about to happen. This is the entry point for Bitcoin. I previously wrote about a pattern that I'd like to reiterate: Bitcoin tends to follow major price movements in gold, but with more significant gains. When gold rises due to concerns about currency devaluation, Bitcoin eventually follows, and usually outperforms gold. It's not an immediate synchronization, but the correlation is undeniable in the long run. Why? Because gold and Bitcoin share a crucial characteristic in a devaluing world: neither can be arbitrarily created by governments or central banks. Gold's supply is limited by geological conditions and mining costs. Bitcoin's supply is limited by its protocol: only 21 million Bitcoins will ever be created. That's all; no emergency Federal Reserve meeting can print more Bitcoins. But even more interesting is this: Bitcoin is a superior hard currency to gold. This might be controversial for gold enthusiasts; for Bitcoin supporters, it's a consensus. But for those unfamiliar with Bitcoin, let's explain why this is true from a data perspective. Gold supply increases by approximately 1.5%-2% annually through mining; its scarcity is relative, not absolute. A fixed amount of gold will enter circulation each year for the foreseeable future. Bitcoin's supply, however, follows a fixed decreasing schedule, with the block reward halving approximately every four years. There will always be 21 million Bitcoins. As of December 2025, approximately 19.96 million have been mined, representing over 95% of the final supply. The remaining approximately 1.04 million (about 5% of the final supply) will be mined within the next century. After that, new coin issuance will be zero. No new Bitcoins will be created. Bitcoin is the highest quality hard currency in human history. For thousands of years, gold has successfully protected wealth during every currency devaluation. When Rome devalued silver coins, gold preserved its value; when the Weimar Republic over-issued marks, gold preserved its value. Since Nixon closed the gold window in 1971, the price of gold per ounce has risen from $35 to over $4,000. Gold's mission is to preserve value, and it does it very well. But what about Bitcoin? Bitcoin has the potential to significantly boost purchasing power because it combines gold's monetary attributes with qualities gold never possessed: absolute scarcity and a new adoption curve. Bitcoin is currently lagging behind gold. Let's look at 2025. Gold rose over 60% last year, breaking all-time highs multiple times, with strong central bank buying. The signals of devaluation are extremely strong. Meanwhile, Bitcoin has fallen back below $90,000, down more than 25% from its October high of over $125,000. Bitcoin has significantly lagged behind gold's recent performance. Why this disconnect? Because many investors, especially traditional financial allocators, still view Bitcoin primarily as a "risk asset" rather than a monetary asset. When liquidity concerns intensify or markets shift towards risk aversion, Bitcoin often falls before or along with tech stocks and other speculative assets. Despite fundamentally different value propositions, this reflexive behavior persists. The result is that Bitcoin's price action remains noisy and volatile, heavily influenced by short-term liquidity flows. It reacts to Fed announcements, stock market sentiment, and risk appetite, unlike gold, which has a history of over 5,000 years. But this is precisely where the opportunity lies: this cognitive gap is temporary, and this creates opportunities for asymmetric returns. As more institutions, sovereign wealth funds, and central banks understand Bitcoin's monetary attributes, the label of "risk asset" will gradually fade. Bitcoin will increasingly be traded as a true store of value, rather than the speculative tech bet many still perceive it to be. Until then, every sell-off triggered by risk aversion presents an opportunity for long-term investors who understand this logic. You are buying an asset with the monetary attributes of gold at a discount, and that discount comes from those who haven't yet grasped its value. History shows that when gold rises due to monetary policy concerns, Bitcoin eventually follows suit, and often with greater gains. There are four reasons: 1. Superior monetary attributes: Absolute scarcity outweighs relative scarcity. 2. Earlier adoption curve: Smaller market capitalization means potentially larger percentage gains. 3. Additional utility: Payment networks, programmable money, self-custodial sovereign attributes. 4. Network effects: Value grows exponentially with adoption. XI. Asymmetric Opportunities The data is compelling. At current prices, the total value of globally mined gold is approximately $30 trillion. It is a mature asset, widely held by households, institutions, and central banks, and has served as a store of value for millennia. Bitcoin, on the other hand, has a market capitalization of approximately $2 trillion. But Bitcoin is only 16 years old. If Bitcoin captures only a fraction of the gold premium as institutions and sovereign nations adopt it as a reserve asset, its upside potential will be substantial. A simple calculation: if Bitcoin's market capitalization grows to half that of gold ($15 trillion), its current price would increase 7-8 times; if it reaches the equivalent of gold's $30 trillion, its current price would increase 15-17 times. More importantly: there are strong reasons to believe that Bitcoin's monetary premium should eventually surpass that of gold, as Bitcoin is objectively a superior hard currency with attributes that gold cannot match.

The Great Divergence: The Aftermath of a $100 Investment in 1971 (Nixon's Abolition of the Gold Standard)

Consider the advantages Bitcoin offers that gold cannot match:

Absolute Scarcity: Exactly 21 million, not an approximation.

Portability: For just a few dollars, $1 billion worth of Bitcoin can be transferred to any corner of the globe in 10 minutes. Transferring the equivalent value of gold, even just a few miles, is prohibitively logistical and costly.

**Forfeiture Resistance:** Properly stored Bitcoin cannot be confiscated. Cross-border asset transport can be extremely risky; just ask those fleeing hostile environments or whose cash or even gold bars have been confiscated at the border. **Verifiability:** Cryptographic verification is more convenient and reliable than testing the purity and weight of gold. **Gold is a safe, proven, and time-tested choice. It is unassuming, reliable, and stable, protecting your purchasing power during periods of depreciation. **Bitcoin, on the other hand, presents an asymmetric opportunity. It possesses all the monetary attributes of gold, plus additional advantages, and is in an earlier adoption phase. Its risk/reward ratio is extremely attractive for investors with a long-term perspective. **Some savvy investors have not only recognized this but have also invested their capital.**

12. The Institutional Shift Has Already Happened

Five years ago, Bitcoin was called "rat poison squared" by Warren Buffett and "fraud" by Jamie Dimon (CEO of JPMorgan Chase). It was seen as a fringe asset for speculators and criminals.

Now? The shift is astonishing.

Vanguard, one of the most conservative asset management firms in the U.S., with $9.3 trillion in assets under management, has just opened its platform to Bitcoin ETFs. After refusing to allow clients to participate in spot Bitcoin ETF trading for 691 days, the company has completely changed its stance. The former CEO, who once called cryptocurrencies "too volatile and speculative," has left the company, and the new CEO, Salim Ramji, was a BlackRock executive who helped launch the world's largest Bitcoin ETF (IBIT).

This is no coincidence; it's a signal.

JPMorgan Chase, the bank whose CEO once called Bitcoin a "fraud," is now allowing institutional clients to use Bitcoin as collateral for loans. In 2026, you will be able to apply for lines of credit with Bitcoin holdings at one of the largest banks in the US. The bank will also launch crypto-asset-backed lending products with loan-to-value ratios of up to 25%. Morgan Stanley will also open Bitcoin trading to E*Trade clients in 2026. This isn't limited to US institutions. The Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund tripled its Bitcoin holdings in the third quarter of 2025. The Abu Dhabi Investment Board increased its IBIT holdings by 230%, from 2.4 million shares to nearly 8 million shares, worth over $517 million. Their statement to Bloomberg explicitly stated, "We view Bitcoin as a store of value similar to gold, and we believe it will play an increasingly important role alongside gold as the world continues to move towards a more digital future." Please read this again: A sovereign wealth fund managing over $1.7 trillion in assets explicitly categorizes Bitcoin and gold in the same way. And then there's Harvard University. The Harvard endowment currently holds 6.8 million shares of the BlackRock Bitcoin ETF (IBIT), making it the fund's largest publicly disclosed equity holding. This holding is accelerating, with an additional 4.9 million shares added in the third quarter of 2025 alone. They didn't just dabble; they nearly tripled their holding in a single quarter. At $50 per IBIT, this equates to $340 million in Bitcoin exposure. One of the world's most prestigious and conservative institutional investors holds Bitcoin as its largest equity holding and is actively increasing its position. Note: None of these institutions are "trading" Bitcoin. They are not seeking quick profits, but rather making strategic allocation decisions based on long-term portfolio construction. This is the shift: from a trading instrument to a strategic asset class.

Institutional Adoption Curve: From "Fraud" to Fiduciary Responsibility in 8 Years

"When institutions enter the game, the rules change—they control trillions of dollars in capital."

We are witnessing a typical technology adoption curve in real time:

1. Ridicule ("Bitcoin is a tool used by drug dealers")

2. Denial ("This is a bubble that will eventually burst")

3. Acceptance ("Perhaps there is some value in it")
